evidence_span: Birds are the reservoir hosts of WNV.
source_citation: WHO West Nile virus fact sheet
notes: Group-level bird reservoir evidence; useful for role logic but not a species-level assignment.

evidence_span: Humans, horses, and other mammals are "dead end" hosts.
source_citation: CDC West Nile Virus Key Messages
notes: Explicit CDC source language supports a human dead-end/incidental host role.

evidence_span: Horses, just like humans, are dead-end hosts.
source_citation: WHO West Nile virus fact sheet
notes: WHO source language is at common-name horse level; matched here to domestic horse candidate.

evidence_span: Sylvatic yellow fever cycles involve virus transmission between non-human primates and forest mosquitoes.
source_citation: CDC Transmission of Yellow Fever Virus
notes: Group-level primate reservoir evidence; do not propagate to every primate species without species-specific support.

evidence_span: Urban yellow fever transmission occurs between humans and urban mosquitoes primarily Aedes aegypti.
source_citation: CDC Transmission of Yellow Fever Virus
notes: Human role is cycle-specific for urban transmission and should not be generalized to sylvatic maintenance.

evidence_span: Humans with dengue virus can infect mosquitoes during the viremic period.
source_citation: WHO Dengue and severe dengue fact sheet
notes: Supports the urban human-mosquito-human cycle; does not assign non-human reservoirs.

evidence_span: Yersinia pestis is maintained naturally in a cycle involving wild rodents and fleas.
source_citation: CDC How Plague Spreads
notes: Group-level rodent maintenance evidence; do not assign every rodent species as a reservoir or maintenance host.

evidence_span: Plague epizootics can spill over to incidental hosts including humans.
source_citation: CDC MMWR plague treatment guidelines
notes: Human role is incidental or spillover except pneumonic human-to-human transmission contexts.

evidence_span: RVF transmission can amplify in naive ruminants via local competent mosquitoes.
source_citation: WHO Rift Valley fever fact sheet
notes: Group-level livestock amplification evidence; candidate cattle sheep goats camels and buffalo need species-specific review before species assignments.

evidence_span: RVF primarily affects animals but can infect humans and most human infections result from infected animal tissues or mosquito bites.
source_citation: WHO Rift Valley fever fact sheet
notes: Human role is spillover/incidental rather than maintenance.

evidence_span: Mosquitoes become infected when they feed on viremic humans or non-human primates which are likely the main amplifying reservoirs.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Chikungunya
notes: Human role is strongest for urban human-mosquito-human transmission.

evidence_span: Mosquitoes become infected when they feed on viremic humans or non-human primates which are likely the main amplifying reservoirs.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Chikungunya
notes: Group-level primate reservoir or amplification evidence; do not assign local primate species automatically.

evidence_span: In the urban cycle Aedes mosquitoes bite infected people and then transmit Zika virus to uninfected people.
source_citation: CDC Transmission of Zika Virus
notes: Human role is urban-cycle amplification and does not settle sylvatic reservoir candidates.

evidence_span: Sylvatic rodents are the primary reservoir hosts for enzootic VEEV strains.
source_citation: Venezuelan Equine Encephalitis - StatPearls
notes: Group-level enzootic reservoir evidence; species-level rodent assignments need source-specific review.

evidence_span: Equine vaccination removes the most important vertebrate amplifying host from the epizootic transmission cycle.
source_citation: CDC MMWR Update Venezuelan Equine Encephalitis Colombia 1995
notes: Common equine amplification evidence mapped to domestic horse candidate.

evidence_span: VEE outbreaks cause human cases but control focuses on equine amplification and mosquito transmission.
source_citation: CDC MMWR Venezuelan Equine Encephalitis Colombia 1995
notes: Humans can have high viremia in epidemic contexts so keep incidental role under review rather than final dead-end language.

evidence_span: Livestock can remain asymptomatic and act as amplifying hosts of Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus.
source_citation: CDC Emerging Infectious Diseases CCHF Virus in Cattle and Ticks Israel
notes: Group-level livestock amplification evidence; local cattle sheep goat camel and buffalo candidates need species review.

evidence_span: Humans are infected by tick bites or contact with infected animal blood or tissues.
source_citation: WHO Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever fact sheet
notes: Human role is spillover/incidental with additional nosocomial human-to-human risk.

evidence_span: WHO states ostriches are susceptible and may show high infection prevalence in endemic areas.
source_citation: WHO Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever fact sheet
notes: Ostrich row is susceptible host support not a reservoir or broad bird proxy.

evidence_span: CCHFV serology reviews and exposure models support wild ungulates as exposed tick-feeding hosts in endemic foci.
source_citation: Seroepidemiological Studies of Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever Virus in Domestic and Wild Animals | Animal Exposure Model for Mapping Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever
notes: Group-level susceptible-host support for ungulates; do not promote individual wild species to reservoir or amplifying roles.

evidence_span: Small rodents are the primary amplifying host for tick-borne encephalitis virus.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Tick-Borne Encephalitis
notes: Group-level row because there are no current local host candidate rows for TBE in the role surface.

evidence_span: TBE virus is maintained between Ixodes ticks and small rodents and transmitted to humans by infected ticks.
source_citation: CDC Transmission of Tick-borne Encephalitis
notes: Human row added as source-backed spillover evidence even though no local host candidate row exists.

evidence_span: People can acquire TBE by ingesting unpasteurized dairy products from infected cows goats or sheep.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Tick-Borne Encephalitis
notes: Foodborne infected-dairy evidence supports susceptible/spillover livestock context only; not reservoir or amplifier evidence.

evidence_span: People can acquire TBE by ingesting unpasteurized dairy products from infected cows goats or sheep.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Tick-Borne Encephalitis
notes: Foodborne infected-dairy evidence supports susceptible/spillover livestock context only; not reservoir or amplifier evidence; goat-associated transmission is most commonly reported.

evidence_span: People can acquire TBE by ingesting unpasteurized dairy products from infected cows goats or sheep.
source_citation: CDC Yellow Book Tick-Borne Encephalitis
notes: Foodborne infected-dairy evidence supports susceptible/spillover livestock context only; not reservoir or amplifier evidence.

evidence_span: Natural infections of SFTSV occur in several domesticated animal hosts in endemic areas.
source_citation: CDC Emerging Infectious Diseases SFTSV among Domesticated Animals China
notes: Host-presence evidence only; does not assign reservoir or amplifier role.

evidence_span: SFTS is a tick-borne zoonosis and rare human-to-human transmission has been documented.
source_citation: UKHSA SFTS epidemiology outbreaks and guidance
notes: Human spillover role with rare secondary transmission caveat; no local host candidate row exists.

evidence_span: In the sylvatic cycle non-human primates sloths and perhaps birds serve as vertebrate hosts.
source_citation: WHO Oropouche virus disease fact sheet
notes: Group-level and partly uncertain reservoir evidence; do not assign local primate or sloth species automatically.

evidence_span: PAHO describes humans as acting as the amplifying host in Oropouche virus transmission with Culicoides paraensis and Culex quinquefasciatus as possible vectors.
source_citation: PAHO Oropouche epidemiological update
notes: Human amplifying role should be reviewed because source is an outbreak update and vector ecology remains incomplete.

evidence_span: Scientists believe African fruit bats are involved in the spread of orthoebolaviruses and may be the source of the virus.
source_citation: CDC How Ebola Disease Spreads
notes: Likely reservoir group evidence; do not assign individual bat species without source-specific support.

evidence_span: Ebola virus can enter the human population through contact with infected animals such as chimpanzees gorillas and monkeys.
source_citation: WHO Ebola disease fact sheet
notes: Non-human primates are treated as spillover or source animals rather than natural reservoirs.

evidence_span: Ebola spreads through human-to-human transmission via direct contact with blood or body fluids of infected people.
source_citation: WHO Ebola disease fact sheet
notes: Human outbreak amplification role; not a reservoir assignment.

evidence_span: For ebolaviruses it is not known what animal carries them in nature but scientists suspect that bats are likely involved.
source_citation: CDC Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers About
notes: Sudan-virus-specific reservoir is uncertain; keep group-level and low confidence.

evidence_span: Once filoviruses are introduced into the human population they can spread between people through contact with infected body fluids.
source_citation: CDC Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers About
notes: Human outbreak amplification role for filovirus transmission.

evidence_span: Rousettus aegyptiacus bats are considered natural hosts for Marburg virus.
source_citation: WHO Marburg virus disease fact sheet
notes: Species-level reservoir evidence matches local candidate.

evidence_span: Human-to-human Marburg transmission can occur through contact with infected bodily fluids.
source_citation: Marburg Virus Disease - StatPearls
notes: Human outbreak amplification role; not reservoir.

evidence_span: Fruit bats of the Pteropodidae family are the natural host of Nipah virus.
source_citation: WHO Nipah virus fact sheet
notes: Group-level reservoir evidence; local Pteropus species should not be assigned without species-specific support.

evidence_span: Transmission to humans can occur from direct contact with infected animals like bats pigs or horses and past pig-farm outbreaks required animal movement restrictions and culling.
source_citation: WHO Nipah virus fact sheet
notes: Domestic pig intermediate/amplifying-host role mapped to local pig candidate.

evidence_span: WHO notes the need to mitigate human-to-human transmission in health-care facilities.
source_citation: WHO Nipah virus fact sheet
notes: Human role is context-dependent and should not be treated as reservoir.

evidence_span: Mastomys rats serve as the main reservoir of Lassa virus.
source_citation: WHO Lassa fever fact sheet
notes: Species-level reservoir evidence matches local Mastomys natalensis candidate.

evidence_span: WHO highlights limiting human-to-human transmission in healthcare facilities for Lassa fever management.
source_citation: WHO Lassa fever fact sheet
notes: Human-to-human transmission exists but is context-dependent.

disease_name: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (Hantaan virus)
evidence_span: The reservoir host of Hantaan virus is the striped field mouse Apodemus agrarius.
source_citation: CDC Emerging Infectious Diseases HFRS in US Soldiers South Korea
notes: Species-level Hantaan reservoir evidence matches local candidate.

disease_name: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (Hantaan virus)
evidence_span: HFRS is caused by hantaviruses and people are infected from rodent-associated exposure rather than serving as a maintenance host.
source_citation: CDC HFRS Clinician Brief
notes: Human spillover/incidental role for Hantaan/HFRS.

disease_name: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (Hantaan virus)
evidence_span: PLOS NTD reports HTNV antibodies and RNA in Apodemus chejuensis on Jeju Island and frames A. chejuensis-borne HTNV as a potential etiologic agent of HFRS in southern Korea
source_citation: Park et al 2021 PLOS NTD A novel genotype of Hantaan orthohantavirus harbored by Apodemus agrarius chejuensis

evidence_span: Argentine hemorrhagic fever is spread by Calomys rodents and the animal reservoir is prevalent in the endemic Pampas region.
source_citation: UKHSA Argentine haemorrhagic fever guidance
notes: Species-level reservoir assignment uses local Calomys musculinus candidate.

evidence_span: Canada PSDS names Calomys laucha among the principal JUNV reservoirs, while the Mills et al. field study found far fewer antigen-positive C. laucha than C. musculinus and points to C. musculinus as the primary human-exposure reservoir.
source_citation: Canada Junin virus pathogen safety data sheet | Mills et al. 1992 Junin virus longitudinal study
notes: source_check_candidate_id=candidate_046; caveat=secondary or minor reservoir support; keep separate from primary C. musculinus and do not generalize to all Calomys or other rodents.

evidence_span: Human-to-human transmission of Argentine hemorrhagic fever is very rare and may occur via direct contact with infected body fluids.
source_citation: UKHSA Argentine haemorrhagic fever guidance
notes: Human role is primarily spillover/incidental rather than reservoir.

evidence_span: CDC describes wild aquatic birds including gulls terns shorebirds waterfowl ducks geese and swans as natural hosts for bird flu viruses.
source_citation: CDC Bird Flu Current Situation in Wild Birds
notes: Group-level reservoir evidence only; do not assign every bird species from this row.

evidence_span: WHO describes A(H5N1) viruses causing hundreds of millions of poultry infections and recurrent epizootics in poultry populations.
source_citation: WHO Influenza avian and other zoonotic fact sheet
notes: Domestic poultry role is outbreak amplification rather than natural reservoir.

evidence_span: WHO reports A(H5N1) spread to dairy cattle in the United States in 2024 with some exposed dairy farm workers infected.
source_citation: WHO Influenza avian and other zoonotic fact sheet
notes: Cattle row records recent mammalian host evidence and should not be treated as reservoir.

evidence_span: WHO states human infections with avian and zoonotic influenza viruses are rare and current zoonotic influenza viruses have not demonstrated sustained human-to-human transmission.
source_citation: WHO Influenza avian and other zoonotic fact sheet
notes: Human role is sporadic zoonotic infection from animal exposure.

evidence_span: WHO states the natural reservoir is unknown while various small mammals are susceptible.
source_citation: WHO Mpox fact sheet
notes: Do not assign reservoir_host because the reservoir remains unknown.

evidence_span: WHO states mpox spreads from person to person mainly through close contact with someone who has mpox.
source_citation: WHO Mpox fact sheet
notes: Human outbreak transmission supports amplifying host role.

evidence_span: CDC states non-human primates can get sick with monkeypox and have signs of disease like humans.
source_citation: CDC Monkeypox in Animals and Pets
notes: Susceptibility evidence only; not reservoir evidence.

evidence_span: Scientific Reports identifies P. conspicillatus with P. alecto as the likely natural reservoir species based on field survey evidence for infectious-virus shedding in urine; CFSPH notes P. conspicillatus appears infected and/or shedding more often than P. poliocephalus or P. scapulatus.

evidence_span: Peel et al. detected HeV-g2 in individual and pooled flying-fox urine samples including P. poliocephalus evidence, and concluded P. alecto and P. poliocephalus excrete HeV-g2 in urine and are likely competent reservoir hosts; this does not make P. poliocephalus a primary HeV-g1 reservoir.
source_citation: Peel et al. 2022 Novel Hendra Virus Variant Circulating in Black Flying Foxes and Grey-Headed Flying Foxes, Australia

evidence_span: Scientific Reports treats P. scapulatus as susceptible but incidental relative to P. alecto/P. conspicillatus reservoir species; CFSPH notes Hendra virus has been detected in all four Australian flying fox species but less often in P. scapulatus than in the main shedding species.
